figures.caption: Figure 2: Scaling law validation and extrapolation. (a) Transfer-Aware allocation achieves the steepest scaling (β = 0.425) and lowest MAE loss across all training budgets, outperforming Data-Proportional by up to 58%. (b) The scaling law fitted on {10K, 50K, 100K} extrapolates to the held-out 200K budget within 1.2% error. (c) Per-domain predicted vs. observed loss at B = 200K shows strong agreement across all six domains (r = 0.989).
Table 6: Domain-specific intensity preprocessing. All domains share the same spatial preprocessing (RAS orientation, 1.5mm isotropic resampling, foreground cropping, 963 patching).

Figure 1: Observations motivating the transfer-aware scaling law. (a) Per-domain MAE loss follows Li = Ei + Ci/T βi with domain-specific exponents varying 3.5× (β = 0.21 for BRAIN T1 to β = 0.76 for HN PET), indicating that domains benefit from additional data at fundamentally different rates. (b) Cross-domain transfer obeys the same power-law form with a directional discount τij : ABD CT transfers strongly to ABD MRI (τ=0.56) but weakly to HN PET (τ=0.04), and the reverse ABD MRI → ABD CT (τ=0.42, dashed) is weaker than the forward direction. (c) Full 6×6 transfer matrix τij (from source j to target i) estimated from pure-domain proxy runs. The matrix is asymmetric (τij ̸= τji) and reveals hub-and-island structure: ABD CT (hub) transfers broadly to most domains, while HN PET (island) is largely isolated.

Table 1: Effective budget decomposition under Transfer-Aware allocation at T=100K. The effective budget reaching target domain i is Teff,i = ∑K j=1 τij ·hj ·T , where each source domain j contributes τij ·hj ·T effective samples. Self-contributions (τii=1) are shown in bold. ABD_CT (hub, h∗=23.7%) provides the largest cross-domain contribution to four targets. HEAD_PET (island, h∗=56.3%) receives almost no incoming transfer and relies on its large direct allocation. Floor domains (h∗=5%) achieve 3.7–6.1× amplification through incoming transfer.

Table 4: Pretraining domain statistics. “Available” refers to the total unlabeled volumes in the source dataset; “Selected” is the number subsampled for our experiments. All volumes are resampled to 1.5mm isotropic resolution.
Table 5: Downstream finetuning datasets. Segmentation tasks report Dice (%); classification tasks report accuracy and AUC. All finetuning protocols are held fixed across pretraining strategies.

Figure 4: Per-domain scaling laws (log-log) comparing transfer-aware and data-proportional allocation. Transferaware achieves a steeper scaling exponent β on all 6 domains, with the largest gains on HEAD PET (β : 0.724 vs. 0.466) and ABD MRI (β : 0.487 vs. 0.352). BRAIN T1 and BRAIN T2 show the smallest differences, consistent with these domains being near saturation at low β values. The 3.5× heterogeneity in β across domains (0.209–0.724) confirms that a single shared scaling exponent, as assumed by prior work, is insufficient for heterogeneous medical data mixtures.

Figure 5: Floor constraint ablation. (a) As the floor ϵ increases, the optimizer loses flexibility: HEAD PET allocation drops from 56.3% to 37.5% and ABD CT from 33.7% to 12.5%. (b) Mean MAE loss is minimized at ϵ=5%; higher floors force budget into domains that receive sufficient signal through transfer, degrading overall performance. (c) Per-domain losses confirm that ϵ=5% achieves the best or near-best loss on every domain simultaneously.
